takeaway: Basedash defines unit economics as the metrics that "measure the profitability of a single customer: the revenue they generate over their lifetime (LTV) versus the cost to acquire them (CAC)." It frames why this matters before scaling: they answer "the most fundamental business question — do you make more money from a customer than it costs to acquire and serve them? If the answer is no, growing faster just means losing money faster."

Wikipedia defines recurring revenue as "revenue that is likely to continue to be generated regularly for a significant period of time," typically from subscriptions or service contracts such as monthly phone plans that guarantee revenue for the contract duration.

Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) defines ARR as "a measure of revenue components that are recurring in nature" that "should exclude one-time (non-recurring) fees and professional service fees," and insists CAC "should be the full cost of acquiring users, stated on a per user basis" while LTV is "the present value of the future net profit from the customer over the duration of the relationship." It gives monthly unit churn as "lost customers/prior month total."

Wikipedia defines average revenue per user (ARPU) as "a measure used primarily by consumer communications, digital media, and networking companies, defined as the total revenue divided by the number of subscribers." Because subscriber counts move daily, analysts divide total revenue for a period by the average number of units in that period.

Wikipedia defines gross margin as "the difference between revenue and cost of goods sold (COGS), divided by revenue," expressed as Gross margin % = (Revenue − COGS) / Revenue × 100%. It notes the distinction that "gross profit" is an absolute monetary amount while "gross margin" is the percentage or ratio.

Corporate Finance Institute defines the Gross Margin Ratio as "a profitability ratio that compares the gross margin of a company to its revenue," computed as (Revenue – COGS) / Revenue. A higher ratio indicates the company retains more profit from each sales dollar after covering the cost of goods sold — a signal of better profitability and cost efficiency.

Wikipedia defines contribution margin as "the selling price per unit minus the variable cost per unit" (C = P − V). It separates contribution from gross margin: a contribution calculation "seeks to separate out variable costs... from fixed costs... on the basis of economic analysis of the nature of the expense, whereas gross margin is determined using accounting standards."

Shopify frames the practical split: contribution margin "measures variable costs of producing a product or service, such as sales commissions" and "can also help a business owner decide which product lines to invest in and which to cut," while gross margin "accounts for any costs directly involved in producing and distributing what a business sells, providing an overall sense of potential profitability." Contribution margin is per-product; gross margin is whole-business.

CFI defines customer acquisition cost (CAC) as "the cost related to acquiring a new customer," computed as CAC = Sales and Marketing Expenses ÷ Number of New Customers. The metric helps a business evaluate how efficiently it is spending to bring on new customers.

Wikipedia defines customer lifetime value (LTV) as "an estimation and prediction of the net profit that a customer contributes to during the entire future relationship with a business." A common simplified formula is (Average Monthly Revenue per Customer × Gross Margin per Customer) ÷ Monthly Churn Rate — e.g. $100 monthly spend × 25% margin ÷ 5% churn = $500 LTV.

Wikipedia defines churn rate (also called attrition or customer turnover) as the metric measuring "the proportion of individuals... customers, or items that leave a defined group during a specified period of time." Crucially, "a customer lifetime can be calculated as the inverse of that customer's predicted churn rate" — a 25% annual churn implies a 4-year average lifetime (1 ÷ 0.25 = 4).

Paddle defines the LTV:CAC ratio as the figure that "determines the long-term value of acquiring a customer," found by dividing lifetime value by acquisition cost. It gives the rule of thumb: "The benchmark LTV:CAC ratio is 3:1. That means if you pay $4,200 to acquire each customer at an LTV of $12,600, each acquired user is worth 3x what you paid to earn their business."

Airtree Ventures defines the CAC payback period as the measure of "how long it takes for you to earn enough gross profit from a customer to pay back the S&M costs of acquiring them," computed as CAC ÷ (Monthly Recurring Revenue × Gross Margin). Its B2B benchmarks: under 12 months selling to SMEs, under 18 to mid-market, under 24 for enterprise accounts.

Stripe defines net revenue retention (NRR) as the metric reflecting "a company's ability to retain and grow revenue from its existing customer base over a given time period," and gross revenue retention (GRR) as the percentage of recurring revenue retained "excluding any upsell, cross-sell, or expansion revenue." GRR caps at 100% because it only subtracts losses; "an NRR over 100% suggests that growth from upsells and expansions exceeds lost revenue."

Wikipedia defines burn rate as "the rate at which a company consumes its cash," typically expressed monthly and used for startups. Combined with available funds, it "provides a time measure to when the next funding event needs to take place" — the practical basis for calculating runway.

Wall Street Prep states the Rule of 40 as the rule that "at scale, the combined value of revenue growth rate and profit margin should exceed 40% for healthy SaaS companies," expressed as Recurring Revenue Growth Rate (%) + EBITDA Margin (%). It credits venture capitalist Brad Feld with popularizing the metric for evaluating SaaS company health.

Bessemer Venture Partners introduces the Rule of X as "an adjusted form of Rule of 40 taking composition (and weighting) into account," arguing that "for late-stage cloud companies, revenue growth should be weighted 2-3x more than FCF margin when assessing valuation." It was created by Byron Deeter and Sam Bondy to "more accurately represent a cloud business's valuation."
